blocks.content: Spark has three physical join strategies. Picking the right one — or forcing it — is the single fastest win in Spark performance tuning. Every Data Engineer at MAANG is expected to explain all three in an interview.

rows: Zero — small table copied to every executor
High on driver + executor (small table fits in RAM)
Both sides large; default when BHJ not possible
Two full shuffles + sort on both sides
Low per-executor — streams rather than holds all data
One side significantly smaller, but over broadcast threshold
One shuffle — smaller side built into a hash map
Medium — smaller partition must fit in executor RAM

blocks.content: Rule of thumb for interviews: BHJ = fastest (0 shuffles). SMJ = safest (works at any scale). SHJ = middle ground that Spark rarely picks automatically.

Catalyst uses table statistics to decide. The key threshold is spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold. If estimated size of one join side is below this limit, Spark broadcasts it. Set to -1 to disable broadcasting entirely.

# Check current threshold (default = 10 MB = 10485760 bytes)
spark.conf.get('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold')
# → '10485760'

# Raise threshold to 100 MB (safe for cluster with 8 GB+ executor RAM)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', 100 * 1024 * 1024)

# Disable auto-broadcast (force SMJ for all joins)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', -1)

# Check the plan — does it say BroadcastHashJoin?
df_large.join(df_small, 'id').explain(mode='formatted')

Statistics are only accurate if you've run ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS (Hive metastore) or the table has Parquet/Delta min/max stats. Without stats, Spark may underestimate size and skip broadcasting a table that should be broadcast.

sections.title: Forcing a Join Strategy with Hints

blocks.content: When Spark makes the wrong choice — or you know more than the optimizer — use join hints. Hints override the statistics-based decision.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# ── Method 1: broadcast() function (Python-friendly) ──
result = df_large.join(broadcast(df_small), 'dept_id')

# ── Method 2: SQL hint syntax ──
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(d) */ e.name, d.dept_name
    FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id
""")

# ── Method 3: DataFrame hint ──
result = df_large.hint('broadcast').join(df_small, 'dept_id')

# Force Sort-Merge Join (disable BHJ even for small tables)
result = df_a.hint('merge').join(df_b, 'key')

# Available hints: BROADCAST, MERGE (SMJ), SHUFFLE_HASH, SHUFFLE_REPLICATE_NL

sections.title: Bucketing: Eliminating Shuffle on Repeated Joins

blocks.content: Bucketing pre-distributes data by a join/group key at write time. When two bucketed tables join on the same key with the same bucket count, Spark skips the shuffle entirely — the data is already co-located.

# Write tables as bucketed Parquet (one-time cost)
df_orders.write \
    .bucketBy(200, 'customer_id') \
    .sortBy('customer_id') \
    .saveAsTable('orders_bucketed')

df_customers.write \
    .bucketBy(200, 'customer_id') \
    .sortBy('customer_id') \
    .saveAsTable('customers_bucketed')

# Future joins skip shuffle!
ordersB = spark.table('orders_bucketed')
customersB = spark.table('customers_bucketed')
ordersB.join(customersB, 'customer_id').explain()
# Physical plan shows: SortMergeJoin WITHOUT Exchange nodes → zero shuffle ✅
MAANG pattern: Dimension tables used in thousands of daily jobs are almost always bucketed. The one-time write cost pays off after just a few reads.

rune_cards.definition: Copies the smaller table to every executor. Zero shuffles required. Fastest join but limited to small tables (default ≤10 MB). Force with broadcast() or /*+ BROADCAST */.
Default join for large tables. Requires two full shuffles (both sides repartitioned by join key) plus a sort. Spill-safe — doesn't require the full partition to fit in RAM.

spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold — estimated size in bytes below which Spark will broadcast a relation. Default 10 MB. Set to -1 to disable. Raise to 256 MB+ for larger lookup tables if RAM permits.

Pre-partition data at write time by a column into N buckets. When two tables share the same bucket key + count, joins and aggregations skip the shuffle entirely. Requires Hive metastore (saveAsTable).

quiz.question: You have a 5 MB lookup table joined to a 500 GB fact table. Which join strategy does Spark pick by default and why?
options: Sort-Merge Join — it's always the default
Broadcast Hash Join — the lookup table is below autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (10 MB)
Shuffle Hash Join — one side is much smaller
Cartesian Join — no join key specified
quiz.feedback: Correct. The 5 MB table is under the 10 MB default threshold, so Catalyst broadcasts it to all executors. This results in zero shuffles on the 500 GB fact table.

quiz.question: Your plan shows two Exchange nodes before a SortMergeJoin. You know the right side is only 80 MB. What's the fastest fix?
options: Wrap the right-side DataFrame with broadcast()
quiz.feedback: wrap the right side with broadcast(df_right). This tells Catalyst to copy it to every executor and eliminates both shuffles. The other options don't change the join strategy.
